validation.summary: 13/13 journal durability, crash, recovery, retention, schema, index, event, and no-replay tests passed after the atomic-hook repair.
43/43 T005-T008 Kernel, journal, event, lock, and state tests passed together.

validation.command: real default withMutationLocks + journal proof probe
validation.summary: A journaled task.start completed under real operation/project/task/run-index locks, persisted succeeded, and released every lock.
Handlers listed, read, and reconciled the original record without invoking mutation work.
Syntax check passed for 65 JavaScript files after the final change.
204 total; 203 passed; 1 existing POSIX platform skip; 0 failures; 126.4 seconds.
No trailing whitespace or tracked-diff whitespace errors in T007/T008 paths.

review.evidence: The review verified one authoritative atomic record per operation, rebuildable state-free indexes, existing operation-first lock semantics with persisted lock proof, terminal-only pruning, indefinite unresolved retention, bounded fail-closed diagnosis of newer records, and recovery APIs that accept evidence callbacks but never an original mutation handler. Existing registry/run/runtime/ownership authorities remain unchanged and the full regression passes.

obligation_evidence.evidence: The Application Kernel owns journal transitions while existing handlers and control-plane stores remain the only resource-effect and resource-state authorities.
operation.list/get/reconcile are storage/evidence primitives; recover never accepts or invokes the original mutation handler and missing IDs cannot create work.
All post-dispatch fault points retain the original prepared/running/terminal record and expose only recovery, never a second execution or fallback.
Kernel persists prepared, then running, before invoking the handler; the handler test observes running before its first declared effect.
Running/indeterminate records reconcile through evidence-only callbacks, and handler/effect counters remain unchanged during recovery.
Persistence and result semantics distinguish recoverable idempotency from indeterminate effects and make no universal exactly-once claim.
Same ID/same digest returns the original record; different digest refuses; record revisions and immutable fields prevent a second prepared identity.
Indeterminate work cannot transition to running, unresolved records never age-prune, and public recovery handlers contain no replay path.
